title: Scampi
subtitle: Langoustine tails coated in seasoned panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden
excerpt: Scampi uses the three-step breading process (flour, egg, breadcrumbs) to coat langoustine tails before frying. The panko crust fries to a light, open golden shell around the sweet, firm shellfish. The method takes 30 minutes including the preparation.
sourceNotes: British pub and restaurant tradition. Scampi in the British sense refers specifically to deep-fried breaded langoustine or prawns, documented on British pub and restaurant menus from the 1960s onward. The dish was popularised when frozen scampi became widely available. The homemade version using fresh langoustine is the superior form.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bread the scampi up to 1 hour ahead and keep refrigerated on a lined tray.
recipe.temperatureNote: Fry in oil at 180°C on the hob; no oven temperature required.

glossaryTerms.definition: The three-step coating process: dredge in flour, dip in beaten egg, then press into breadcrumbs. The flour helps the egg adhere; the egg binds the breadcrumbs. Called à l'anglaise in French, this technique is used for schnitzels, fish goujons, and scampi.

content.text: Scampi made with fresh langoustine tails bears little resemblance to the frozen versions found in pub freezers. The shell stays crisper and lighter because the meat inside is firmer and releases less moisture during frying. The three-step coating (flour, egg, panko) produces a clean, open crust that fries golden in under 3 minutes.

Set up three shallow bowls: one with the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour seasoned with salt, one with the beaten {{eggs}} eggs, and one with the {{breadcrumbs-panko}} of panko breadcrumbs mixed with the cayenne and a pinch of salt. This is the

Pat the {{langoustine-tails}} of langoustine tails dry with kitchen paper. Working one at a time, dredge each tail in flour (shaking off the excess), dip in egg, then press firmly into the panko, coating all sides. Place on a lined tray.

Pour the {{vegetable-oil}} of vegetable oil into a large heavy saucepan to a depth of at least 8 cm. Heat to 180°C. Fry the scampi in batches of 6 to 8 for 2 to 3 minutes until deep golden all over. Do not crowd the pan. Drain on a wire rack and season immediately with salt.
Serve immediately with lemon wedges. Scampi does not hold; it softens within a few minutes of coming out of the oil.

items.symptom: Breadcrumb coat falls off during frying
items.cause: Langoustine not dried before breading, or the flour stage was skipped.
items.fix: Pat completely dry before the flour stage. Press the breadcrumbs firmly onto each tail so they adhere before frying.
Check the oil is at 180°C before each batch. Let it return to temperature between batches.

items.cause: Overcooked. Langoustine tails cook through in 2 to 3 minutes at 180°C.
items.fix: Remove from the oil as soon as the crust is golden. The shellfish continues to cook in the residual heat.

content.text: Scampi in the British sense refers to deep-fried breaded langoustine, not the Italian scampi al limone from which the name derives. It became a British pub standard from the 1960s onward, when frozen scampi made from processed langoustine tails was widely distributed to pubs and restaurants. The frozen version uses reformed or pre-minced shellfish; the fresh version is a quite different thing. Langoustines from Scottish and Irish waters are among the finest shellfish available in Britain, and fresh scampi made from them is one of the better things the British pub kitchen can produce.
